papers.title: Research on Optimal Scheduling of Grid-Side Energy Storage Based on Reinforcement Learning
papers.venue: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Automation, Electronics and Electrical Engineering (AUTEEE)

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/AUTEEE67053.2025.11322291?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/AUTEEE67053.2025.11322291, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. papers.abstract: The rising penetration of distributed photovoltaic (PV) generation in urban distribution networks has introduced key operational challenges—such as feeder capacity constraints, renewable curtailment, and load volatility—that hinder progress toward carbon-neutral power systems. Energy storage systems (ESS) offer an effective solution by mitigating these issues through strategic charge–discharge scheduling. However, traditional optimization-based methods struggle with the uncertainties and dynamic complexities of real-world grids, including stochastic loads, intermittent renewable, and time-varying electricity prices. This paper proposes a data-driven adaptive framework using Deep Q-Network (DQN) reinforcement learning for optimal grid-side energy storage scheduling. The problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) with a twelve-dimensional state vector capturing time features, price signals, load–PV dynamics, and the battery’s state of charge (SOC). A multi-objective reward function is designed to jointly optimize economic arbitrage under time-of-use (TOU) pricing and grid stability via peak shaving, while enforcing battery safety constraints. The framework’s three-layer architecture—covering data generation, environment simulation, and intelligent decision-making—enables robust testing under varied scenarios. Simulation results show that the DQN strategy reduces electricity purchase costs, cuts PV curtailment, and lowers the peak-to-valley load gap by 28.1%, keeping SOC within safe limits. The agent autonomously learns effective coordination strategies, such as energy reserve preparation for evening peaks and opportunistic PV absorption at midday. Overall, this study demonstrates the feasibility and superiority of deep reinforcement learning for intelligent energy storage management in distribution networks with high renewable penetration and dynamic operating conditions.
